read-history-with-log-and-show.label: Read the History You Built
read-history-with-log-and-show.summary: Inspect the community-project history and committed file content without changing state.

sketch0.title: History Is a Sequence of Snapshots

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Read the Three-Commit Timeline
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt: The guided repository now has three commits. Read the concise history in newest-first order and leave the repository unchanged.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Use the compact history view as a read-only timeline.

help.concept: git log --oneline summarizes each recorded snapshot with its subject.
help.hint_1: The newest subject should be Update community contacts.
help.hint_2: The oldest subject should be Start community project.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared schedules and contact notes.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.instructions: The guided repository now has three commits. Read the concise history in newest-first order and leave the repository unchanged.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared schedules and contact notes.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0100
' > community-project/contacts.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Old planning notes

Spring flyer draft.
' > community-project/old-notes.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project/generated
printf %s 'Generated summary

Created automatically from project notes.
' > community-project/generated/summary.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Start community project'
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Update community schedule'
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0130
' > community-project/contacts.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-03T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-03T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Update community contacts'

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you read the newest-first concise timeline?

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Inspect the Latest Snapshot Summary
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt: Inspect the commit currently named by `HEAD` with a one-line subject and file statistics. Confirm that the newest snapshot changed `contacts.txt`.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Ask Git for the current commit summary, not the working-tree diff.

help.concept: HEAD identifies the currently checked-out commit.
help.hint_1: The subject should be Update community contacts.
help.hint_2: The stat summary should name contacts.txt.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.instructions: Inspect the commit currently named by `HEAD` with a one-line subject and file statistics. Confirm that the newest snapshot changed `contacts.txt`.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Read a File from the Latest Snapshot
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt: Read `contacts.txt` directly from `HEAD` and confirm that the committed content includes `Help desk: 555-0130`. Do not open or modify the working copy.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Use the commit-and-path form of the history inspection command.

help.concept: git show can read a file exactly as it exists in a committed snapshot.
help.hint_1: HEAD is the latest community-project commit.
help.hint_2: The path is contacts.txt relative to the repository root.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.instructions: Read `contacts.txt` directly from `HEAD` and confirm that the committed content includes `Help desk: 555-0130`. Do not open or modify the working copy.

Did you read the committed contact file directly from `HEAD`?

single-choice-1.title: HEAD in Git
single-choice-1.prompt: What does `HEAD` identify in this course?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Scan the repository timeline, open one checkpoint, understand `HEAD`, and move between overview and detail.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

multi-choice-1.title: Understanding git log --oneline
multi-choice-1.hint: Choose the one answer that describes this command or concept precisely.

help.concept: Scan the repository timeline, open one checkpoint, understand `HEAD`, and move between overview and detail.
help.hint_1: Remove choices that act on a different repository layer or promise behavior Git does not provide.
help.hint_2: Prefer the exact definition used in this chapter over a nearby Git idea.

options.a: A full line-by-line diff for every commit
options.b: A compact one-line summary of each commit
options.c: Only the files currently in the staging area
options.d: Only the untracked files in the working tree

multi-choice-2.title: Purpose of git show
multi-choice-2.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git show` command?
multi-choice-2.hint: Choose the one answer that describes this command or concept precisely.

help.concept: Scan the repository timeline, open one checkpoint, understand `HEAD`, and move between overview and detail.
help.hint_1: Remove choices that act on a different repository layer or promise behavior Git does not provide.
help.hint_2: Prefer the exact definition used in this chapter over a nearby Git idea.

options.a: To list every commit as one short line
options.b: To display the details of a selected commit
options.c: To initialize an ordinary project folder

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of Git Commands
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the following Git commands in the order you would use them to inspect a commit's history and details.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Scan the repository timeline, open one checkpoint, understand `HEAD`, and move between overview and detail.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

sketch0.title: History Is a Sequence of Snapshots
sketch0.bodyMarkdown: `git log --oneline` lists the newest commit first. Short identifiers are useful references, but the course does not grade exact hashes because valid repositories can produce different values.

sketch1.title: HEAD Names the Current Snapshot
sketch1.bodyMarkdown: `HEAD` points to the currently checked-out commit. `git show --stat --oneline HEAD` combines its subject with a compact file summary.

sketch2.title: Show Can Read Committed Text
sketch2.bodyMarkdown: `git show HEAD:path` reads a text file from the committed snapshot without changing the working tree. History inspection should leave status unchanged.
